summary: Katerina Breshkovsky, known as the Little Grandmother, was a tall, broad woman with kind eyes who visited Hull-House when she was sixty-five. She spent thirty-three years in Siberia as a political prisoner because she taught Russian peasants to read and write, which the Czar forbade. She believed literacy would open the gates to the past and future for the peasants, and she wanted Americans to understand the struggles in Russia. After sharing her story at Hull-House, she returned to Russia and was again sent to Siberia.
cues: teaching peasants to read and write

summary: Hull-House was a vibrant settlement house on Halsted Street in Chicago that attracted attention from wealthy society leaders curious about why anyone would choose to live there. It was a place where notable visitors like Katerina Breshkovsky and Peter Kropotkin came to share their stories and ideals. I remember standing in front of Hull-House on a hot day when Jane Addams welcomed Prince Kropotkin, much to the excitement of the children gathered there. Hull-House was a hub for immigrants and children from diverse backgrounds, fostering community and education.

Siberia was where Katerina Breshkovsky spent thirty-three years as a political prisoner because she taught peasants to read and write, which was considered a crime by the Russian Czar. It was a harsh place used to punish those who challenged the regime, like Katerina. She told us that despite the suffering, her time in Siberia strengthened her resolve to fight for literacy and freedom for the Russian people. Siberia represented both punishment and resistance in her life story.

The Russian Empire was the oppressive regime under which Katerina Breshkovsky was imprisoned for educating peasants. The Czar did not want the peasants to know their history or think about the future, which is why teaching literacy was a crime. Peter Kropotkin, another notable Russian figure, also visited Hull-House representing the ideals of democracy and reform from Russia. The Russian Empire's harsh policies deeply affected many, including those who sought freedom and education.

Jane Addams was the founder and heart of Hull-House, welcoming important visitors like Peter Kropotkin and Katerina Breshkovsky. I remember her stopping at the desk to announce the arrival of the prince, which excited all the children waiting outside. She was a leader who bridged cultures and brought the community together, guiding us through the visits of these great figures. Jane Addams made Hull-House a place of learning, hope, and connection in Chicago.

Peter Kropotkin, known as Prince Kropotkin of Russia, visited Hull-House and was greeted by a crowd of excited children who imagined him as a real prince. He was a short, mound-shouldered man with a thick half-gray beard, carrying a valise and wearing a shabby coat, but he was a great Russian democrat. Jane Addams introduced him to the children, who formed a guard of honor for him despite their poverty. His visit symbolized the connection between Russian democratic ideals and the immigrant community in Chicago.

Chicago was the city where Hull-House stood, a melting pot of immigrants from Irish, Italian, Jewish, German, Russian, Polish, Bohemian, Greek, French, Swedish, and Norwegian backgrounds. I grew up playing on the streets near West Taylor and Throop Streets, surrounded by friends from many cultures. Chicago was a place of both hardship and community, where people like Jane Addams worked to improve lives and where visitors like Katerina Breshkovsky and Peter Kropotkin shared their stories. The city was alive with the hopes and struggles of future American citizens.

Halsted Street was the location of Hull-House, a neighborhood many found dirty and dusty, which made some visitors uncomfortable. I remember a shiny carriage stopping there, and a woman complaining about the bad air, saying a prince wouldn't come to such a street. Despite this, Hull-House on Halsted Street was a vibrant center for immigrants and reformers, attracting people like Katerina Breshkovsky and Peter Kropotkin. It was a place where community and education thrived amidst the rough surroundings.

West Taylor Street, near Throop Street, was where an abandoned convent stood surrounded by a diverse immigrant community including Irish, Italian, Jewish, German, Russian, Polish, Bohemian, Greek, French, Swedish, and Norwegian families. As children, we played there and shared friendships despite teasing each other with ethnic nicknames. The convent was soon to be sold to Jewish philanthropists who planned to establish the Chicago Hebrew Institute there, which sparked ghost stories among the neighborhood children. West Taylor Street was a lively place full of culture and stories.

Throop Street was close to West Taylor Street where the old convent stood, surrounded by a mixed immigrant neighborhood. It was part of the area where children like me played and shared stories, including the spooky ghost sightings in the convent windows. The convent was about to be converted into the Chicago Hebrew Institute by Jewish philanthropists, which caused some local rumors and superstitions. Throop Street was part of the vibrant, multicultural fabric of our neighborhood in Chicago.

The Chicago Hebrew Institute was planned to be housed in an abandoned convent on West Taylor Street near Throop Street, purchased by Jewish philanthropists. This plan sparked ghost stories among the local children and neighbors, who said the saints in the convent were unhappy about the building being sold to Jews. My mother even spoke with the caretaker to learn the truth behind the 'ghosts,' discovering it was just him checking the windows with a candle. The Institute represented a new chapter for the convent and the Jewish community in our diverse Chicago neighborhood.
